episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [TechRadar] 'Who wouldn't want a large-scale industrial polluter sitting in their backyard?': California residents ban data centers as public backlash grows and [Android Authority] Walmart’s Onn accidentally leaks its cheaper alternative to Google Nest Cam with Floodlight, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How should communities balance AI data center development promises against environmental and landscape protection, and who should have decision-making authority? Topics include: Grid capacity and water demand impacts of data center expansion, Overstated permanent employment claims in tech industry siting proposals, Tŝilhqot'in Aboriginal title as legal protection mechanism, Regulatory capacity gaps in BC industrial approvals, Landscape and cultural value as unformalized stakes in approvals processes.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Cariboo Chilcotin Conservation Society

articles.title: [Business Insider] Kevin O'Leary says he's 'not walking away' from his Utah AI data center, despite call to shrink it by 75%
articles.summary: A state Senator proposed a 75% cut and environmental restrictions on Kevin O'Leary's Stratos data center Project. O'Leary said he's not walking away.

discussion.central_question: How should communities balance AI data center development promises against environmental and landscape protection, and who should have decision-making authority?
discussion.riley_position: Data centers are inevitable and will be sited somewhere; the strategic approach is to negotiate better conditions and leverage Indigenous title as a more durable protection mechanism than municipal bans, rather than refuse engagement entirely.
riley_key_evidence: International Energy Agency revised forecasts showing data centers will account for meaningful share of global electricity demand through end of decade
Rural data center builds in Washington/Oregon generated 30-50 permanent positions for tens of megawatts
Tŝilhqot'in Aboriginal title decision (2014) established legal jurisdiction requiring consent, not just consultation
discussion.casey_position: Tech industry systematically overstates job creation and environmental benefits while avoiding costs; regulatory capacity in BC is insufficient to extract meaningful concessions, and landscape value rarely has formal standing in approvals processes.
casey_key_evidence: Satya Nadella's 'lower environmental impact' claims lack transparent baseline comparisons
Permanent job numbers are poor relative to capital investment compared to what gets sold in pitch decks
BC environmental assessment process has had mixed record on extracting meaningful concessions
discussion.resolution: They converged on the necessity of Indigenous governance frameworks as the structural lever, acknowledged a capacity gap requiring further direct engagement with Tŝilhqot'in Government, and agreed the actionable approach is tracking First Nation positions and demanding specific accountability in public comment processes.
topics_covered: Grid capacity and water demand impacts of data center expansion
Overstated permanent employment claims in tech industry siting proposals
Tŝilhqot'in Aboriginal title as legal protection mechanism
Regulatory capacity gaps in BC industrial approvals
Landscape and cultural value as unformalized stakes in approvals processes
